family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: High hemoglobin can be a sign of underlying lung or heart problems, or come from dehydration, smoking, or living at high altitude. Signs can include headache, dizziness, vision trouble, slurred speech, and a reddened face. Source: testing.com
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: Low hemoglobin means anemia — the blood is not carrying enough oxygen to the body's organs and muscles. For an 86-year-old bedridden cancer patient, this means his heart and brain are getting less oxygen than they need, making him feel extremely tired, weak, and breathless even while lying still. Source: cancer.gov (Anemia and Cancer Treatment — NCI)
family_guidance.affected_system: Red blood cells and oxygen delivery to every organ. In elderly patients, the heart and brain are most sensitive to low oxygen levels and will show symptoms first. Source: cancer.gov (Anemia — NCI)
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: Extreme tiredness, shortness of breath even lying still, pale or yellowish skin, dizziness, fast heartbeat, headaches, fainting. Also watch his stool closely: bright red blood, or black sticky tar-like stool, means the tumor may be bleeding — report it. Source: cancer.gov, cancer.org
family_guidance.warning_signs_tonight: Alert the nurse immediately if he suddenly cannot catch his breath, becomes very pale, feels his heart racing or pounding, faints, or is impossible to wake up fully. Source: cancer.gov (Anemia — NCI)
family_guidance.critical_threshold: If he shows extreme shortness of breath at rest, rapid heart rate, or fainting — alert the nurse immediately. The doctor will decide if a blood transfusion is needed. Source: cancer.gov (Anemia and Cancer Treatment — NCI)
family_guidance.foods_to_give: Offer foods high in iron and protein: eggs, lean red meat, chicken, fish, dark leafy greens (like spinach), beans and lentils. If he cannot eat solid food, offer oral nutrition supplements such as Ensure or Boost. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer — NCI)
family_guidance.foods_to_avoid: Avoid alcohol, carbonated drinks, very spicy or acidic foods (tomatoes, citrus) that are hard to digest. Do not give aspirin or ibuprofen without doctor approval — these can cause stomach bleeding. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer — NCI)
family_guidance.hydration_guidance: Offer fluids between meals rather than during meals so his stomach does not feel too full to eat. Aim for at least 8 cups of fluid per day. Soups and broths count. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer — NCI)
family_guidance.refusal_handling: Do not force food, drink, or medications. Offer very small amounts — a few spoonfuls, a few sips — more frequently rather than three large meals. If he refuses solid food, try liquid options: milkshakes, smoothies, soup broth, or oral supplements like Ensure or Boost. If he refuses medication, tell the nurse right away so the care team can decide the next step. At advanced stages, do not force eating or drinking as this can cause discomfort. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer — NCI)
family_guidance.bedridden_risks: Low hemoglobin combined with immobility greatly increases the risk of pressure sores because less oxygen reaches the skin. Reposition him every 1 to 2 hours. Protect his heels, tailbone, and hips with pillows. Watch for red or purple skin over bony areas and alert the nurse immediately. Also, low blood oxygen raises the risk of confusion (delirium). Source: mskcc.org (Preventing Pressure Injuries), healthinaging.org (Skin Problems)
family_guidance.supplements_safety: Do NOT give over-the-counter iron pills. In cancer with inflammation the body blocks iron absorption (a hormone called hepcidin), so the pills do not raise his blood — but they cause severe constipation, which is dangerous with a colon tumor (risk of bowel blockage), AND they turn the stool black, hiding the signs of internal bleeding. Iron should only be given by IV if the doctor prescribes it after tests. No herbal supplements or vitamins without the doctor. Source: cancer.org, mskcc.org
family_guidance.immune_and_hygiene: All family members must wash hands thoroughly with soap and warm water before entering his room and after any contact. Anyone with a cold, cough, fever, or any illness must not visit. Avoid bringing in raw or undercooked food from outside. Keep the room clean. Limit the number of visitors at one time. Do not give him fever-reducing medicine (paracetamol, ibuprofen) without asking the nurse first — it can hide signs of a serious infection. Source: cancer.gov (Infection and Neutropenia — NCI)
family_guidance.communication_if_confused: Speak in short, simple sentences — one topic at a time. Use a calm, soft, soothing voice. Give him time to respond. If he is confused or scared, gently remind him where he is and that you are there to help. Do not argue, correct, or raise your voice. If he is focused on something upsetting, redirect the conversation to something comforting. Acknowledge his feelings rather than dismissing them. Alert the nurse immediately if confusion is sudden, severe, or new. Source: mskcc.org (Caring for Someone With Delirium)
family_guidance.emotional_support: Sit with him quietly and let him know you are there — your presence matters more than words. Do not pressure him to eat, talk, or feel positive. Let him direct what he needs. Share simple family news to keep him connected. Avoid sharing distressing news or discussing all his test numbers in detail. If he asks how he is doing, be honest but calm and focus on what the doctors are doing to help him. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer, Fatigue and Cancer)
family_guidance.oncologist_questions: Ask: 'Is his hemoglobin low enough to need a blood transfusion now?' and 'What hemoglobin level would make you decide to transfuse?' and 'Is this anemia caused by the cancer, the treatment, or blood loss?' Source: cancer.gov (Anemia — NCI)
family_guidance.treatment_impact: Very low hemoglobin is a common reason chemo is delayed. Doctors usually transfuse blood when hemoglobin drops below about 8, or sooner if he has chest pain, breathlessness at rest, or fainting. The blood is carefully matched first (this takes 2-4 hours) and given slowly over 2-4 hours with close monitoring, especially in an older patient. Source: cancer.org, mskcc.org
family_guidance.palliative_care: If his hemoglobin continues to fall despite transfusions and his overall condition is declining, this may be a time to discuss comfort-focused care goals with the medical team. Ask the doctor: 'At what point would transfusions no longer help him feel better?' Source: cancer.gov (End-of-Life Care for People Who Have Cancer — NCI)

family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: A high hematocrit means the blood has too many red blood cells or is too concentrated. Common causes include dehydration, lung disease, heart problems, certain kidney tumors, smoking, or rare blood disorders. Source: testing.com
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: PCV (hematocrit) measures what percentage of blood is made of red blood cells. A low PCV means anemia — the same problem as low hemoglobin — and has the same effect: less oxygen reaching his organs, causing extreme fatigue and weakness. Source: cancer.gov (Anemia — NCI)
family_guidance.affected_system: Red blood cells and oxygen delivery to all organs. Source: cancer.gov (Anemia — NCI)
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: Same as low hemoglobin: extreme tiredness, shortness of breath, pale skin, dizziness, rapid heartbeat. Source: cancer.gov (Anemia — NCI)
family_guidance.warning_signs_tonight: Sudden shortness of breath at rest, very pale skin, racing heartbeat, or fainting — alert nurse immediately. Source: cancer.gov (Anemia — NCI)
family_guidance.critical_threshold: Alert nurse if he is breathless at rest or shows signs of heart strain. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.foods_to_give: Iron-rich and protein-rich foods: eggs, lean meat, fish, dark leafy greens, beans, Ensure/Boost. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.foods_to_avoid: Avoid alcohol, aspirin, ibuprofen, carbonated or acidic drinks. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.hydration_guidance: Offer fluids between meals. At least 8 cups per day. Soups and broths count. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.refusal_handling: Do not force food, drink, or medications. Offer very small amounts — a few spoonfuls, a few sips — more frequently rather than three large meals. If he refuses solid food, try liquid options: milkshakes, smoothies, soup broth, or oral supplements like Ensure or Boost. If he refuses medication, tell the nurse right away so the care team can decide the next step. At advanced stages, do not force eating or drinking as this can cause discomfort. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer — NCI)
family_guidance.bedridden_risks: Being completely bedridden raises three key risks regardless of this test result: (1) Pressure sores — reposition him every 1 to 2 hours, protect heels, tailbone, and hips with pillows, watch for red or purple skin over bony areas and alert the nurse immediately if found. Do not massage reddened areas. Keep skin clean and dry. (2) Blood clots in the legs (DVT) — do not massage his legs; ask the nurse about compression stockings and gentle foot exercises he can do in bed. (3) Chest infection (pneumonia) — ask the nurse about repositioning him upright at times to help his breathing. Source: mskcc.org (Preventing Pressure Injuries), healthinaging.org (Caregiver Guide: Skin Problems)

Iron only by IV if the doctor prescribes. No supplements without the doctor. Source: cancer.org, mskcc.org

family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: A high RBC count means there are more red blood cells than normal. This can be caused by dehydration, smoking, low oxygen levels, certain cancers, or some medications. Source: testing.com
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: Low red blood cell count means anemia — fewer cells available to carry oxygen through the body. For an elderly cancer patient, this means less energy, more fatigue, and greater mental confusion risk. Source: cancer.gov (Anemia — NCI)
family_guidance.affected_system: Oxygen transport to all organs. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: Extreme tiredness, pale skin, dizziness, shortness of breath, rapid heartbeat. Source: cancer.gov (Anemia — NCI)
family_guidance.warning_signs_tonight: Alert nurse if breathless at rest, very pale, fainting, or heart racing. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.critical_threshold: Same as Hb — extreme breathlessness or fainting = call nurse immediately. Source: cancer.gov

family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: High platelets can occur as a reaction to infection or inflammation — in his case likely related to his active infection. Currently in normal range.
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: Low platelet count (thrombocytopenia) means the blood cannot clot properly, raising the risk of dangerous bleeding. The risk of bleeding goes up when platelets drop below 20,000. A platelet transfusion may be needed if they fall below 10,000. Source: cancer.org (Low Platelet Count — American Cancer Society)
family_guidance.affected_system: Blood clotting. Platelets are the small cells that form clots to stop bleeding.
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: Watch for: unusual bruising, bleeding gums, nosebleeds, blood in urine or stools (dark/black stools), very small red dots on the skin (petechiae), or prolonged bleeding from any cut. Source: cancer.org (Low Platelet Count)
family_guidance.warning_signs_tonight: Alert the nurse immediately if he has a nosebleed that won't stop, blood in urine or stools, sudden severe headache, or any active uncontrolled bleeding. Source: cancer.org (Low Platelet Count)
family_guidance.critical_threshold: If platelets fall below 10,000, a transfusion is typically given. Any active bleeding with a low platelet count = call the nurse immediately. Source: cancer.org (Low Platelet Count — American Cancer Society)
family_guidance.foods_to_give: High-protein foods to support bone marrow health. Eggs, chicken, fish, beans. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.foods_to_avoid: Do NOT give aspirin, ibuprofen, or any NSAID pain relievers — these impair platelet function and increase bleeding risk. Source: cancer.org (Low Platelet Count)
family_guidance.hydration_guidance: Normal hydration. Offer at least 8 cups of fluid daily. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.refusal_handling: Do not force food, drink, or medications. Offer very small amounts — a few spoonfuls, a few sips — more frequently rather than three large meals. If he refuses solid food, try liquid options: milkshakes, smoothies, soup broth, or oral supplements like Ensure or Boost. If he refuses medication, tell the nurse right away so the care team can decide the next step. At advanced stages, do not force eating or drinking as this can cause discomfort. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer — NCI)
family_guidance.bedridden_risks: Being bedridden with low platelets means even small skin injuries from pressure can lead to bruising or bleeding. Reposition gently — do not drag him across sheets. Use a draw sheet for safe transfers. Check skin carefully during repositioning. Source: healthinaging.org (Skin Problems), mskcc.org (Preventing Pressure Injuries)
family_guidance.supplements_safety: Do not give fish oil, vitamin E, garlic supplements, or any blood-thinning herbal products — these can worsen bleeding risk with low platelets. Source: cancer.org (Low Platelet Count)
family_guidance.immune_and_hygiene: All family members must wash hands thoroughly with soap and warm water before entering his room and after any contact. Anyone with a cold, cough, fever, or any illness must not visit. Avoid bringing in raw or undercooked food from outside. Keep the room clean. Limit the number of visitors at one time. Do not give him fever-reducing medicine (paracetamol, ibuprofen) without asking the nurse first — it can hide signs of a serious infection. Source: cancer.gov (Infection and Neutropenia — NCI)
family_guidance.communication_if_confused: Speak in short, simple sentences — one topic at a time. Use a calm, soft, soothing voice. Give him time to respond. If he is confused or scared, gently remind him where he is and that you are there to help. Do not argue, correct, or raise your voice. If he is focused on something upsetting, redirect the conversation to something comforting. Acknowledge his feelings rather than dismissing them. Alert the nurse immediately if confusion is sudden, severe, or new. Source: mskcc.org (Caring for Someone With Delirium)
family_guidance.emotional_support: Sit with him quietly and let him know you are there — your presence matters more than words. Do not pressure him to eat, talk, or feel positive. Let him direct what he needs. Share simple family news to keep him connected. Avoid sharing distressing news or discussing all his test numbers in detail. If he asks how he is doing, be honest but calm and focus on what the doctors are doing to help him. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer, Fatigue and Cancer)
family_guidance.oncologist_questions: Ask: 'Is his platelet count stable or dropping?' and 'What level would trigger a platelet transfusion for him?' and 'Are any of his current medications reducing his platelet count?' Source: cancer.org (Low Platelet Count)
family_guidance.treatment_impact: Platelet counts below a safe threshold (often 75,000–100,000) will delay chemotherapy. Source: cancer.org / cancer.net (Managing Side Effects)

family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: A very high white blood cell count means the immune system is actively fighting a serious infection or major inflammation. His WBC was 20.4 (very high) and has now returned to 7.9 (normal) — this is a positive sign the infection is responding to treatment. Source: cancer.gov (Infection and Neutropenia — NCI)
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: A very low white blood cell count (neutropenia) means the body cannot fight infections properly. Even a minor cut or a mild cold can become life-threatening. Source: cancer.gov (Infection and Neutropenia — NCI)
family_guidance.affected_system: The immune system. White blood cells are the body's defense against bacteria, viruses, and other threats. Source: cancer.gov (Infection — NCI)
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: Any sign of infection: fever (38°C/100.4°F or higher), chills, cough, sore throat, diarrhea, mouth sores, redness around any IV line or wound, burning when urinating, or skin rash. Source: cancer.gov (Infection — NCI)
family_guidance.warning_signs_tonight: A fever of 38C (100.4F) or higher is an emergency in a cancer patient — but so is an abnormally LOW temperature below 36C, and sudden confusion or extreme sleepiness, which are often the first signs of infection in the elderly. Alert the nurse IMMEDIATELY. Do NOT give paracetamol or ibuprofen first without calling — it can hide a dangerous infection. Source: cancer.gov, mskcc.org
family_guidance.critical_threshold: Fever of 38°C (100.4°F) or higher + any WBC abnormality = call the nurse immediately. WBC below 1,500 (absolute neutropenia) = extreme infection risk requiring isolation precautions. Source: cancer.gov (Infection — NCI), cancer.net search results (ASCO)
family_guidance.foods_to_give: Cook all meat, fish, and eggs thoroughly — raw food carries bacteria that are dangerous when immunity is low. High-protein foods: eggs, well-cooked chicken, fish, yogurt, beans. Source: cancer.gov (Infection — NCI, Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.foods_to_avoid: Avoid raw or undercooked meat, fish, or eggs. Avoid raw salad from outside the hospital. Avoid unpasteurized dairy products. Source: cancer.gov (Infection — NCI)
family_guidance.hydration_guidance: At least 8 cups of fluid per day. Good hydration supports immune function. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.refusal_handling: Do not force food, drink, or medications. Offer very small amounts — a few spoonfuls, a few sips — more frequently rather than three large meals. If he refuses solid food, try liquid options: milkshakes, smoothies, soup broth, or oral supplements like Ensure or Boost. If he refuses medication, tell the nurse right away so the care team can decide the next step. At advanced stages, do not force eating or drinking as this can cause discomfort. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer — NCI)
family_guidance.bedridden_risks: Low immunity combined with immobility creates high risk of hospital-acquired infections including pneumonia (from lying flat), urinary tract infections, and skin infections at pressure sites. Ask the nurse about measures to prevent each of these in a bedridden patient. Source: cancer.gov (Infection — NCI)
family_guidance.supplements_safety: Do not give echinacea, garlic pills, or any 'immune-boosting' herbal supplements — these can interfere with cancer treatment. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.immune_and_hygiene: His immune system is weak, so prevent infection aggressively: everyone washes hands with soap for 20 seconds before entering; no sick visitors and no one who recently had a live vaccine; give him his OWN towel, cup, and utensils (do not share); wipe high-touch surfaces (door handles, phone, remote, bed rails) daily; do NOT bring fresh flowers, plants, or soil into the room (they carry fungus that can cause pneumonia); cook all meat, fish, and eggs thoroughly and avoid buffets, salad bars, and raw or unpasteurized food; for mouth care use a soft toothbrush and a rinse of 1 teaspoon baking soda + 1 teaspoon salt in 4 cups warm water — never an alcohol mouthwash. Source: cancer.gov, mskcc.org
family_guidance.communication_if_confused: Speak in short, simple sentences — one topic at a time. Use a calm, soft, soothing voice. Give him time to respond. If he is confused or scared, gently remind him where he is and that you are there to help. Do not argue, correct, or raise your voice. If he is focused on something upsetting, redirect the conversation to something comforting. Acknowledge his feelings rather than dismissing them. Alert the nurse immediately if confusion is sudden, severe, or new. Source: mskcc.org (Caring for Someone With Delirium)
family_guidance.emotional_support: Sit with him quietly and let him know you are there — your presence matters more than words. Do not pressure him to eat, talk, or feel positive. Let him direct what he needs. Share simple family news to keep him connected. Avoid sharing distressing news or discussing all his test numbers in detail. If he asks how he is doing, be honest but calm and focus on what the doctors are doing to help him. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer, Fatigue and Cancer)
family_guidance.oncologist_questions: Ask: 'Has his WBC been affected by cancer treatment?' and 'What WBC level would cause you to delay his next treatment?' and 'Should he be on preventive antibiotics?' Source: cancer.gov (Infection — NCI)
family_guidance.treatment_impact: WBC must be in a safe range before chemotherapy. Neutropenia (very low WBC) is one of the most common reasons chemotherapy is delayed or dose-reduced. Source: cancer.gov / cancer.net

family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: High neutrophil percentage means the body is fighting a bacterial infection. This is consistent with the high CRP and was much higher before — it is now trending toward normal. Source: cancer.gov (Infection — NCI)
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: Very low neutrophil percentage means the primary defense against bacteria is severely weakened. Any fever becomes an emergency. Source: cancer.gov (Infection — NCI)
family_guidance.affected_system: Bacterial infection defense. Neutrophils are the first line of response to bacteria.
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: Fever (38°C+), chills, increasing pain, worsening confusion. Source: cancer.gov (Infection — NCI)
family_guidance.warning_signs_tonight: Fever of 38C or higher = call the nurse immediately. Also watch for a LOW temperature below 36C and for sudden confusion — in the elderly these can be the main early signs of infection. Do not give fever medicine first. Source: cancer.gov, mskcc.org
family_guidance.critical_threshold: Fever ≥38°C in a cancer patient = emergency. Source: cancer.gov (Infection — NCI)
family_guidance.foods_to_give: Well-cooked protein: eggs, chicken, fish, yogurt. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.foods_to_avoid: Raw or undercooked food. No unpasteurized products. Source: cancer.gov (Infection — NCI)
family_guidance.hydration_guidance: At least 8 cups of fluid daily. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.refusal_handling: Do not force food, drink, or medications. Offer very small amounts — a few spoonfuls, a few sips — more frequently rather than three large meals. If he refuses solid food, try liquid options: milkshakes, smoothies, soup broth, or oral supplements like Ensure or Boost. If he refuses medication, tell the nurse right away so the care team can decide the next step. At advanced stages, do not force eating or drinking as this can cause discomfort. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer — NCI)
family_guidance.bedridden_risks: Being completely bedridden raises three key risks regardless of this test result: (1) Pressure sores — reposition him every 1 to 2 hours, protect heels, tailbone, and hips with pillows, watch for red or purple skin over bony areas and alert the nurse immediately if found. Do not massage reddened areas. Keep skin clean and dry. (2) Blood clots in the legs (DVT) — do not massage his legs; ask the nurse about compression stockings and gentle foot exercises he can do in bed. (3) Chest infection (pneumonia) — ask the nurse about repositioning him upright at times to help his breathing. Source: mskcc.org (Preventing Pressure Injuries), healthinaging.org (Caregiver Guide: Skin Problems)
family_guidance.supplements_safety: No immune-boosting supplements without doctor approval. Source: cancer.gov

family_guidance.treatment_impact: This test may be ordered regularly when someone is being treated or monitored for a white-blood-cell-related condition. Source: testing.com

family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: High monocyte count may indicate the body is recovering from or actively fighting a chronic infection or inflammation. Currently normal. Source: testing.com (CBC)
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: Rarely significant, but very low counts can be associated with bone marrow issues or certain therapies.
family_guidance.affected_system: Immune system — monocytes clean up infected tissue and coordinate other immune cells.
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: No direct symptoms; monitored alongside WBC and infection markers.
family_guidance.critical_threshold: A large rise or drop in one or more white-cell types usually prompts repeat testing; a result barely outside the normal range may or may not be significant. Source: testing.com
family_guidance.refusal_handling: Do not force food, drink, or medications. Offer very small amounts — a few spoonfuls, a few sips — more frequently rather than three large meals. If he refuses solid food, try liquid options: milkshakes, smoothies, soup broth, or oral supplements like Ensure or Boost. If he refuses medication, tell the nurse right away so the care team can decide the next step. At advanced stages, do not force eating or drinking as this can cause discomfort. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer — NCI)
family_guidance.bedridden_risks: Being completely bedridden raises three key risks regardless of this test result: (1) Pressure sores — reposition him every 1 to 2 hours, protect heels, tailbone, and hips with pillows, watch for red or purple skin over bony areas and alert the nurse immediately if found. Do not massage reddened areas. Keep skin clean and dry. (2) Blood clots in the legs (DVT) — do not massage his legs; ask the nurse about compression stockings and gentle foot exercises he can do in bed. (3) Chest infection (pneumonia) — ask the nurse about repositioning him upright at times to help his breathing. Source: mskcc.org (Preventing Pressure Injuries), healthinaging.org (Caregiver Guide: Skin Problems)
family_guidance.immune_and_hygiene: All family members must wash hands thoroughly with soap and warm water before entering his room and after any contact. Anyone with a cold, cough, fever, or any illness must not visit. Avoid bringing in raw or undercooked food from outside. Keep the room clean. Limit the number of visitors at one time. Do not give him fever-reducing medicine (paracetamol, ibuprofen) without asking the nurse first — it can hide signs of a serious infection. Source: cancer.gov (Infection and Neutropenia — NCI)
family_guidance.communication_if_confused: Speak in short, simple sentences — one topic at a time. Use a calm, soft, soothing voice. Give him time to respond. If he is confused or scared, gently remind him where he is and that you are there to help. Do not argue, correct, or raise your voice. If he is focused on something upsetting, redirect the conversation to something comforting. Acknowledge his feelings rather than dismissing them. Alert the nurse immediately if confusion is sudden, severe, or new. Source: mskcc.org (Caring for Someone With Delirium)
family_guidance.emotional_support: Sit with him quietly and let him know you are there — your presence matters more than words. Do not pressure him to eat, talk, or feel positive. Let him direct what he needs. Share simple family news to keep him connected. Avoid sharing distressing news or discussing all his test numbers in detail. If he asks how he is doing, be honest but calm and focus on what the doctors are doing to help him. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer, Fatigue and Cancer)
family_guidance.oncologist_questions: Questions to ask: what do report terms like "immature granulocytes" or a "left shift" mean, and what other tests might be needed if results are abnormal? Source: testing.com
family_guidance.treatment_impact: This test may be ordered regularly when someone is being treated or monitored for a white-blood-cell-related condition. Source: testing.com

family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: High urea means the kidneys are not clearing protein waste well. Urea comes from the breakdown of protein. A sudden big spike can mean dehydration OR hidden bleeding inside the gut from the colon tumor (digested blood is absorbed as protein and turned into urea). His level has been slowly improving, which is a good sign. Source: testing.com, mskcc.org
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: A low urea (BUN) level can be a sign of malnutrition, not getting enough protein, or liver disease. Source: testing.com
family_guidance.affected_system: The kidneys — which filter blood, control fluid balance, and help regulate blood pressure. Kidney problems affect how medications are cleared from the body, which is critical for cancer treatment dosing.
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: Reduced urine output, swelling in the ankles or feet, nausea, extreme fatigue, confusion, or a strong ammonia smell on his breath. Report any of these to the nurse. Source: testing.com search results
family_guidance.warning_signs_tonight: If he produces very little or no urine, becomes suddenly more confused, his legs swell significantly, or he shows signs of difficulty breathing from fluid buildup — alert the nurse immediately.
family_guidance.critical_threshold: Report immediately if urine output drops to very little or stops. Ask the nurse to monitor and record daily urine output.
family_guidance.foods_to_give: Light, easily digested foods: eggs, small portions of chicken or white fish, yogurt, soft cooked vegetables. Ask the doctor or dietitian what the right protein amount is — too much protein makes kidneys work harder, but too little worsens nutrition. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.foods_to_avoid: Do NOT give NSAID painkillers (ibuprofen, naproxen, high-dose aspirin) — they damage the kidneys and can cause gut bleeding. Avoid pseudoephedrine cold/sinus pills. Very heavy red-meat meals raise urea further, so keep protein high-quality but measured (eggs, poultry, fish) as the dietitian advises. Avoid high-potassium foods unless the doctor approves. Source: mskcc.org, cancer.gov
family_guidance.hydration_guidance: Follow the doctor's exact fluid instructions carefully. Some kidney patients need more fluids to flush out waste; others with fluid retention need less. Do not encourage extra drinking without checking with the nurse first. Source: cancer.gov / kidney research search results
family_guidance.refusal_handling: If he refuses to drink, tell the nurse so IV fluids can be adjusted accordingly. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.bedridden_risks: High urea combined with immobility means medications may build up in the body more than expected, as the kidneys clear them more slowly. Alert the nurse to any unusual sleepiness, confusion, or behavioral changes. Source: kidney function research from cancer.gov-adjacent search results
family_guidance.supplements_safety: Do not give any vitamins, minerals, or herbal teas without doctor approval. High-dose vitamin C, some herbal teas, and turmeric can put extra stress on kidneys. Source: general guidance from cancer.gov
family_guidance.immune_and_hygiene: All family members must wash hands thoroughly with soap and warm water before entering his room and after any contact. Anyone with a cold, cough, fever, or any illness must not visit. Avoid bringing in raw or undercooked food from outside. Keep the room clean. Limit the number of visitors at one time. Do not give him fever-reducing medicine (paracetamol, ibuprofen) without asking the nurse first — it can hide signs of a serious infection. Source: cancer.gov (Infection and Neutropenia — NCI)
family_guidance.communication_if_confused: Speak in short, simple sentences — one topic at a time. Use a calm, soft, soothing voice. Give him time to respond. If he is confused or scared, gently remind him where he is and that you are there to help. Do not argue, correct, or raise your voice. If he is focused on something upsetting, redirect the conversation to something comforting. Acknowledge his feelings rather than dismissing them. Alert the nurse immediately if confusion is sudden, severe, or new. Source: mskcc.org (Caring for Someone With Delirium)
family_guidance.emotional_support: Sit with him quietly and let him know you are there — your presence matters more than words. Do not pressure him to eat, talk, or feel positive. Let him direct what he needs. Share simple family news to keep him connected. Avoid sharing distressing news or discussing all his test numbers in detail. If he asks how he is doing, be honest but calm and focus on what the doctors are doing to help him. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer, Fatigue and Cancer)
family_guidance.oncologist_questions: Ask: 'Is his kidney function stable or still getting worse?' and 'Do any of his current medications need dose adjustments because of his kidney function?' and 'Is there any specific diet restriction for his kidneys?' Source: cancer.gov / kidney research
family_guidance.treatment_impact: High urea and creatinine affect how chemotherapy drugs are dosed. Many chemotherapy agents are cleared by the kidneys — doses may be reduced or drugs changed. Kidney function must be monitored closely before every treatment. Source: search results (prevalence of renal insufficiency in elderly cancer patients)

family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: High creatinine means the kidneys are under stress and are not filtering waste from the blood as well as they should. His creatinine is about twice the normal maximum. It has been slowly improving — which is a positive sign. Source: testing.com (Creatinine Test), cancer.gov
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: A lower-than-expected creatinine may be a sign of malnutrition or loss of muscle mass. Source: testing.com
family_guidance.affected_system: The kidneys. Creatinine is a waste product from normal muscle activity. Healthy kidneys filter it out; high levels mean filtration is impaired. This affects how medications are eliminated from his body.
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: Reduced urine output, swelling in legs or ankles, nausea, fatigue, confusion. Report any of these to the nurse. Source: testing.com search results
family_guidance.warning_signs_tonight: Alert the nurse immediately if: he passes very little or no urine for 8 hours; he gains 1 to 1.5 kg (2-3 lb) in a single day or develops new swelling in the legs or ankles (press the shin 5 seconds — if a dent stays, that is fluid); he cannot breathe lying flat, his chest sounds wet/rattly, or he coughs pink frothy spit (fluid in the lungs — emergency); or he becomes confused. Source: cancer.gov, mskcc.org
family_guidance.critical_threshold: Alert the nurse immediately if urine output drops significantly or stops. Ask the team to monitor urine output daily.
family_guidance.foods_to_give: Light, easily digested protein: eggs, small portions of chicken or fish, yogurt. Ask the dietitian about the right protein level — the kidneys process protein waste. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.foods_to_avoid: Do NOT give NSAID painkillers — ibuprofen (Advil), naproxen (Aleve), celecoxib, or high-dose aspirin. These are the number one preventable cause of sudden kidney failure and also raise the risk of stomach and colon bleeding. Avoid cold/sinus pills with pseudoephedrine. For mild pain, paracetamol (acetaminophen) is usually safer, but only at the dose the doctor allows. Before any CT scan, remind the team about his kidneys — the contrast dye can harm them. Avoid high-potassium foods (banana, orange, potato) unless the doctor approves. Source: mskcc.org, cancer.gov
family_guidance.hydration_guidance: Follow the doctor's fluid instructions exactly. Good hydration helps flush kidneys, but too much fluid can cause swelling if kidneys are failing. Check with the nurse before encouraging extra drinks. Source: cancer.gov / kidney research
family_guidance.refusal_handling: Tell the nurse if he refuses fluids — IV hydration may need to be increased. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.bedridden_risks: Immobility reduces blood circulation to the kidneys. Additionally, medications clear from the body more slowly — raising the risk of side effects from normal doses. Alert the nurse to any unusual drowsiness or behavioral changes. Source: kidney research from search results
family_guidance.supplements_safety: No vitamins, herbal teas, or supplements without doctor approval. High-dose vitamin C, certain herbal teas, and turmeric can worsen kidney function. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.immune_and_hygiene: All family members must wash hands thoroughly with soap and warm water before entering his room and after any contact. Anyone with a cold, cough, fever, or any illness must not visit. Avoid bringing in raw or undercooked food from outside. Keep the room clean. Limit the number of visitors at one time. Do not give him fever-reducing medicine (paracetamol, ibuprofen) without asking the nurse first — it can hide signs of a serious infection. Source: cancer.gov (Infection and Neutropenia — NCI)
family_guidance.communication_if_confused: Speak in short, simple sentences — one topic at a time. Use a calm, soft, soothing voice. Give him time to respond. If he is confused or scared, gently remind him where he is and that you are there to help. Do not argue, correct, or raise your voice. If he is focused on something upsetting, redirect the conversation to something comforting. Acknowledge his feelings rather than dismissing them. Alert the nurse immediately if confusion is sudden, severe, or new. Source: mskcc.org (Caring for Someone With Delirium)
family_guidance.emotional_support: Sit with him quietly and let him know you are there — your presence matters more than words. Do not pressure him to eat, talk, or feel positive. Let him direct what he needs. Share simple family news to keep him connected. Avoid sharing distressing news or discussing all his test numbers in detail. If he asks how he is doing, be honest but calm and focus on what the doctors are doing to help him. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer, Fatigue and Cancer)
family_guidance.oncologist_questions: Ask: 'Is his creatinine stable or still rising?' and 'Which of his current medications need dose adjustment for his kidney function?' and 'Should we start any medication to protect his kidneys?' Source: cancer.gov / research on kidney function in elderly cancer patients
family_guidance.treatment_impact: High creatinine is one of the most important factors in chemotherapy dosing for elderly patients. Many drugs are cleared by the kidneys, and high creatinine means doses must be reduced to prevent toxicity. The oncology team will use his kidney function to calculate safe doses. Source: research showing 66% of elderly cancer patients have impaired renal function

family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: High calcium (hypercalcemia) in cancer patients can be caused by the cancer itself. It can cause confusion, nausea, and kidney problems. Source: search results on hypercalcemia of malignancy
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: Low calcium (hypocalcemia) means the blood does not have enough calcium for normal muscle and nerve function. His calcium is slightly below normal. In elderly patients, this can cause muscle cramps and increased confusion. Source: hypocalcemia in cancer patients research
family_guidance.affected_system: Muscles, nerves, heart rhythm, and bones. Calcium controls muscle contraction and nerve signals.
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: Watch for tingling or numbness around the lips, fingers, and toes (often the first sign), muscle cramps or twitching, and a fluttering or irregular heartbeat. Report these to the nurse. Source: cancer.org, mskcc.org
family_guidance.warning_signs_tonight: Alert the nurse immediately if he develops muscle spasms that he cannot control, tingling around his mouth, or a seizure. If calcium is very low (below 7.0 mg/dL) with these symptoms, this is an emergency. Source: hypocalcemia research
family_guidance.critical_threshold: Calcium below 7.0 mg/dL with muscle spasms or tingling = emergency — alert nurse immediately. Source: hypocalcemia research
family_guidance.foods_to_give: Dairy products if tolerated: milk, yogurt, cheese. Also fortified foods. Ask the doctor if calcium-rich foods are appropriate given his overall condition. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.foods_to_avoid: No specific foods worsen low calcium, but avoid alcohol and excessive caffeine. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.hydration_guidance: Normal hydration. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.refusal_handling: Do not force food, drink, or medications. Offer very small amounts — a few spoonfuls, a few sips — more frequently rather than three large meals. If he refuses solid food, try liquid options: milkshakes, smoothies, soup broth, or oral supplements like Ensure or Boost. If he refuses medication, tell the nurse right away so the care team can decide the next step. At advanced stages, do not force eating or drinking as this can cause discomfort. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer — NCI)
family_guidance.bedridden_risks: Low calcium combined with immobility can worsen muscle weakness and increase delirium risk. Long-term immobility also affects bone calcium balance. Being completely bedridden raises three key risks regardless of this test result: (1) Pressure sores — reposition him every 1 to 2 hours, protect heels, tailbone, and hips with pillows, watch for red or purple skin over bony areas and alert the nurse immediately if found. Do not massage reddened areas. Keep skin clean and dry. (2) Blood clots in the legs (DVT) — do not massage his legs; ask the nurse about compression stockings and gentle foot exercises he can do in bed. (3) Chest infection (pneumonia) — ask the nurse about repositioning him upright at times to help his breathing. Source: mskcc.org (Preventing Pressure Injuries), healthinaging.org (Caregiver Guide: Skin Problems)
family_guidance.supplements_safety: Do NOT buy over-the-counter calcium pills. His total calcium can read low partly because his blood protein (albumin) is low, not always because true calcium is low. Too much calcium can cause kidney stones or a dangerous swing to HIGH calcium (constipation, confusion, heart rhythm problems), and it blocks his other medicines from being absorbed. Any calcium or vitamin D must be set by the doctor. Source: cancer.org, mskcc.org
family_guidance.immune_and_hygiene: All family members must wash hands thoroughly with soap and warm water before entering his room and after any contact. Anyone with a cold, cough, fever, or any illness must not visit. Avoid bringing in raw or undercooked food from outside. Keep the room clean. Limit the number of visitors at one time. Do not give him fever-reducing medicine (paracetamol, ibuprofen) without asking the nurse first — it can hide signs of a serious infection. Source: cancer.gov (Infection and Neutropenia — NCI)
family_guidance.communication_if_confused: Speak in short, simple sentences — one topic at a time. Use a calm, soft, soothing voice. Give him time to respond. If he is confused or scared, gently remind him where he is and that you are there to help. Do not argue, correct, or raise your voice. If he is focused on something upsetting, redirect the conversation to something comforting. Acknowledge his feelings rather than dismissing them. Alert the nurse immediately if confusion is sudden, severe, or new. Source: mskcc.org (Caring for Someone With Delirium)
family_guidance.emotional_support: Sit with him quietly and let him know you are there — your presence matters more than words. Do not pressure him to eat, talk, or feel positive. Let him direct what he needs. Share simple family news to keep him connected. Avoid sharing distressing news or discussing all his test numbers in detail. If he asks how he is doing, be honest but calm and focus on what the doctors are doing to help him. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer, Fatigue and Cancer)
family_guidance.oncologist_questions: Ask: 'Is the low calcium related to his cancer or medications?' and 'Should he have calcium or vitamin D supplementation?' Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.treatment_impact: Electrolyte abnormalities including calcium must be corrected before chemotherapy. Source: cancer.gov

family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: CRP is a protein the liver makes when the body is fighting serious inflammation or infection. His CRP of 130 mg/L is more than 20 times the normal maximum (6 mg/L). This means there is a very active infection or major inflammation happening in his body. The positive news is it has been coming down from 345 — the treatment is working. Source: testing.com (C-Reactive Protein Test), cancer.gov (Infection — NCI)
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: Low or normal CRP is ideal — it means no significant active inflammation or infection. Source: testing.com
family_guidance.affected_system: The whole body's inflammatory response. In his case, the CRP reflects an active infection being treated with antibiotics. Source: testing.com (CRP Test)
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: Fever or chills, increasing pain anywhere in the body, rapid breathing, sudden confusion or agitation, extreme weakness, or looking much worse than before. Source: cancer.gov (Infection — NCI)
family_guidance.warning_signs_tonight: Alert the nurse IMMEDIATELY for any of these: a fever of 38C (100.4F) or higher — OR an abnormally LOW body temperature below 36C (96.8F), which in an elderly patient is just as dangerous as a fever; sudden confusion or extreme sleepiness (delirium is often the FIRST and sometimes only early sign of infection in the elderly); rapid or difficult breathing; cold, clammy, or sweaty skin; or very little urine. Do not give fever medicine before calling the nurse. Source: cancer.gov, mskcc.org
family_guidance.critical_threshold: A fever of 38C or higher, OR a temperature below 36C, in a cancer patient = call the nurse immediately; fever medicine can hide a worsening infection. If he collapses, cannot breathe, or cannot be woken — in Duhok call ambulance 122 and go to Azadi Teaching Hospital or Duhok Oncology Hospital. Source: cancer.gov, mskcc.org
family_guidance.foods_to_give: High-protein, high-calorie foods to support healing and the immune system: eggs, chicken, fish, yogurt, Ensure or Boost if he cannot eat solid food. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer — NCI)
family_guidance.foods_to_avoid: Avoid alcohol, very spicy or acidic foods. Do NOT give ibuprofen or aspirin — these mask infection signs and can harm the stomach and kidneys. Source: cancer.gov (Infection — NCI, Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.hydration_guidance: Encourage fluids as allowed by the care team — water, soup broth, weak tea. Good hydration supports the body's ability to fight infection. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.refusal_handling: If he refuses to drink, offer small sips frequently and report significant refusal to the nurse, as dehydration can worsen infection and fever. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.bedridden_risks: Active infection combined with being bedridden raises the risk of pneumonia from respiratory secretions pooling in the lungs. Ask the nurse about how to safely reposition him to an upright or side-lying position to help his breathing. Reposition every 1 to 2 hours. Source: healthinaging.org (Skin Problems), mskcc.org (Pressure Injuries)
family_guidance.supplements_safety: Do not give any herbal supplements, vitamins, or over-the-counter medicines during an active infection. They can interact with antibiotics or interfere with lab results. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.immune_and_hygiene: With CRP this high indicating active infection, his immune system is severely stressed. All visitors MUST wash hands thoroughly before entering the room. Anyone with a cold, cough, or any illness must not visit. Limit visitors to immediate family only. Keep the room clean. Source: cancer.gov (Infection — NCI)
family_guidance.communication_if_confused: High CRP from active infection is a direct cause of confusion and delirium. Speak in short simple sentences, use a calm and soothing voice, one topic at a time. If he is confused or scared, remind him gently where he is and that you are there to help. Do not argue. Alert the nurse if confusion is new or worsening suddenly. Source: mskcc.org (Caring for Someone With Delirium)
family_guidance.emotional_support: A serious infection is frightening. When talking to him, focus on the positive: the CRP has been falling from 345 to 130, which means the treatment is working. Reassure him calmly without sharing all the numbers. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.oncologist_questions: Ask: 'What is the exact source of the infection causing this high CRP?' and 'What is the treatment plan and how long will antibiotics be needed?' and 'At what CRP level will it be safe to consider other treatments?' Source: cancer.gov (Infection — NCI)
family_guidance.treatment_impact: A CRP this high indicating active serious infection will postpone any chemotherapy or elective procedures until the infection is controlled and CRP returns toward a safer range. Source: cancer.gov / cancer.net
family_guidance.palliative_care: If CRP remains very high despite treatment and his overall condition continues to decline, this may be a time to discuss comfort-focused care goals with the medical team. Source: cancer.gov (Planning the Transition to End-of-Life Care — NCI)

family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: Hypernatremia can be caused by dehydration, losing too much water (e.g., sweating, diarrhea), or a high-sodium diet.
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: Low sodium (hyponatremia) means there is not enough sodium in the blood. Sodium controls fluid balance between cells and the brain. His level of 130.7 is below the safe minimum (136). At his age, even mild low sodium can cause serious confusion, weakness, and fall risk. Source: search results on hyponatremia in elderly cancer patients
family_guidance.affected_system: The brain and nervous system — sodium imbalance directly affects thinking, alertness, balance, and muscle function. Low sodium is one of the most common causes of delirium in elderly hospital patients.
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: Confusion, disorientation, excessive sleepiness, muscle weakness, headache, nausea, unsteadiness (though he is bedridden), or difficulty being woken up. Source: search results on hyponatremia symptoms in elderly
family_guidance.warning_signs_tonight: Alert the nurse IMMEDIATELY if he suddenly becomes more confused, does not recognize family, has a seizure (shaking/convulsions or blank staring), has uncontrollable hiccups, muscle twitching, or cannot be woken normally. Very low sodium can cause seizures and coma. In Duhok, for a seizure or unresponsiveness call ambulance 122 and go to Azadi Teaching Hospital. Source: mskcc.org, cancer.gov
family_guidance.critical_threshold: Sodium below 125 mmol/L can cause seizures and loss of consciousness. Any sudden confusion or inability to wake him = call the nurse immediately. Source: hyponatremia in cancer patients research
family_guidance.foods_to_give: Ask the doctor before adding salty foods — the type of low sodium determines whether salt helps or hurts. Some cases improve with small amounts of salty broth or soup; others require fluid restriction instead. Always follow the doctor's specific instructions. Source: hyponatremia research (treatment varies by type)
family_guidance.foods_to_avoid: Do NOT give extra water or encourage drinking beyond what the doctor ordered. In many cases of low sodium, too much water makes it worse. Source: hyponatremia research — fluid restriction is a common treatment
family_guidance.hydration_guidance: This is the most important and most counter-intuitive point: with low sodium, do NOT give him plain water freely. Plain water dilutes the blood further and can cause dangerous brain swelling (confusion, seizures). Do not leave a water pitcher at the bedside. Follow the doctor's fluid limit exactly — it includes soup, tea, juice, ice cream, and ice chips. For thirst or dry mouth, use ice chips, sugar-free sour candy, dry-mouth swabs, and lip balm. NEVER use a salt substitute (it is potassium and can be deadly with kidney problems). Source: mskcc.org, cancer.gov
family_guidance.refusal_handling: If he refuses his IV treatment or medications, alert the nurse immediately. Correcting sodium is medically critical and must be done under medical supervision. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.bedridden_risks: Low sodium with immobility increases the risk of severe delirium, agitation, and — even in bed — risk of him pulling out IV lines or medical equipment when confused. Stay with him or ensure close nursing supervision if he becomes agitated. Source: mskcc.org (Caring for Someone With Delirium)
family_guidance.supplements_safety: Do not give electrolyte drinks, sports drinks, or sodium supplements without the doctor's instruction. Sodium must be corrected at a medically safe rate — too fast is also dangerous. Source: hyponatremia research
family_guidance.immune_and_hygiene: All family members must wash hands thoroughly with soap and warm water before entering his room and after any contact. Anyone with a cold, cough, fever, or any illness must not visit. Avoid bringing in raw or undercooked food from outside. Keep the room clean. Limit the number of visitors at one time. Do not give him fever-reducing medicine (paracetamol, ibuprofen) without asking the nurse first — it can hide signs of a serious infection. Source: cancer.gov (Infection and Neutropenia — NCI)
family_guidance.communication_if_confused: Low sodium is a direct cause of delirium and confusion. If he is confused: speak in short simple sentences, use a calm soft voice, remind him where he is, do not argue or try to explain the medical situation in detail. Alert the nurse if confusion is sudden or worsening. Source: mskcc.org (Caring for Someone With Delirium)
family_guidance.emotional_support: Sit with him quietly and let him know you are there — your presence matters more than words. Do not pressure him to eat, talk, or feel positive. Let him direct what he needs. Share simple family news to keep him connected. Avoid sharing distressing news or discussing all his test numbers in detail. If he asks how he is doing, be honest but calm and focus on what the doctors are doing to help him. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer, Fatigue and Cancer)
family_guidance.oncologist_questions: Ask: 'What is causing his sodium to be low — is it related to the cancer, medications, or fluids?' and 'How will you correct it, and how quickly?' and 'What sodium level needs to be reached before it is safe to continue other treatments?'
family_guidance.treatment_impact: Low sodium must be corrected to a safe level before chemotherapy or other procedures can proceed safely. Source: general medical context from cancer.gov / research

family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: High potassium (hyperkalemia) can cause dangerous heart rhythm problems. Source: mskcc.org (Potassium Phosphate patient education)
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: Low potassium (hypokalemia) can cause muscle weakness, fatigue, irregular heartbeat, and in severe cases, life-threatening heart rhythm problems. Source: search results on hypokalemia in cancer patients
family_guidance.affected_system: Heart rhythm, muscle function, and nerve signaling. Potassium is critical for a normal heartbeat.
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: Muscle weakness or cramps, extreme fatigue, irregular heartbeat (fluttering sensation in chest), nausea, or constipation. Source: research and mskcc.org search results
family_guidance.warning_signs_tonight: Alert the nurse immediately if he complains of chest fluttering, palpitations, severe muscle cramps, or becomes suddenly very weak. Source: hypokalemia research
family_guidance.critical_threshold: Very low or very high potassium (below 3.0 or above 5.5 mmol/L with symptoms) can cause dangerous heart rhythm problems. Alert the nurse if new symptoms appear. Source: general medical context from cancer research sources
family_guidance.foods_to_give: If potassium is low: foods naturally rich in potassium can help — bananas, oranges, potatoes, avocado, yogurt, and dark leafy greens. Always confirm this with the doctor first. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.foods_to_avoid: If potassium is HIGH: avoid potassium-rich foods (bananas, oranges, potatoes, tomatoes, dark leafy greens) until the doctor says it is safe. Source: mskcc.org search results
family_guidance.hydration_guidance: Normal hydration — follow doctor's instructions. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.refusal_handling: Do not force food, drink, or medications. Offer very small amounts — a few spoonfuls, a few sips — more frequently rather than three large meals. If he refuses solid food, try liquid options: milkshakes, smoothies, soup broth, or oral supplements like Ensure or Boost. If he refuses medication, tell the nurse right away so the care team can decide the next step. At advanced stages, do not force eating or drinking as this can cause discomfort. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer — NCI)
family_guidance.bedridden_risks: Being completely bedridden raises three key risks regardless of this test result: (1) Pressure sores — reposition him every 1 to 2 hours, protect heels, tailbone, and hips with pillows, watch for red or purple skin over bony areas and alert the nurse immediately if found. Do not massage reddened areas. Keep skin clean and dry. (2) Blood clots in the legs (DVT) — do not massage his legs; ask the nurse about compression stockings and gentle foot exercises he can do in bed. (3) Chest infection (pneumonia) — ask the nurse about repositioning him upright at times to help his breathing. Source: mskcc.org (Preventing Pressure Injuries), healthinaging.org (Caregiver Guide: Skin Problems)
family_guidance.supplements_safety: Do not give potassium supplements without a doctor's prescription — too much potassium is as dangerous as too little. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.immune_and_hygiene: All family members must wash hands thoroughly with soap and warm water before entering his room and after any contact. Anyone with a cold, cough, fever, or any illness must not visit. Avoid bringing in raw or undercooked food from outside. Keep the room clean. Limit the number of visitors at one time. Do not give him fever-reducing medicine (paracetamol, ibuprofen) without asking the nurse first — it can hide signs of a serious infection. Source: cancer.gov (Infection and Neutropenia — NCI)
family_guidance.communication_if_confused: Speak in short, simple sentences — one topic at a time. Use a calm, soft, soothing voice. Give him time to respond. If he is confused or scared, gently remind him where he is and that you are there to help. Do not argue, correct, or raise your voice. If he is focused on something upsetting, redirect the conversation to something comforting. Acknowledge his feelings rather than dismissing them. Alert the nurse immediately if confusion is sudden, severe, or new. Source: mskcc.org (Caring for Someone With Delirium)
family_guidance.emotional_support: Sit with him quietly and let him know you are there — your presence matters more than words. Do not pressure him to eat, talk, or feel positive. Let him direct what he needs. Share simple family news to keep him connected. Avoid sharing distressing news or discussing all his test numbers in detail. If he asks how he is doing, be honest but calm and focus on what the doctors are doing to help him. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer, Fatigue and Cancer)
family_guidance.oncologist_questions: Ask: 'Is his potassium level affecting his heart rhythm monitoring?' and 'Does he need potassium supplementation?' Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.treatment_impact: Electrolyte imbalances including potassium must be corrected before chemotherapy. Source: cancer.gov

family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: AST is slightly above normal. AST is found in muscle as well as in the liver. Because his liver-specific tests (ALT, ALP, bilirubin) are all normal, this mild rise is most likely coming from muscle wasting (the body breaking down muscle), NOT from liver damage or cancer spread. It is generally not a cause for panic; doctors worry mainly when AST is 2-3 times above normal. Source: cancer.org, cancer.gov
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: Low AST is normal and healthy — no concern.
family_guidance.affected_system: Liver, heart, and muscle cells. AST is released when these cells are under stress.
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: Yellowing of skin or eyes (jaundice), dark-colored urine (like strong tea), pale or clay-colored stools, right-sided abdominal pain, or severe nausea. Alert the nurse if any of these appear. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.warning_signs_tonight: Jaundice (yellow skin or eyes), very dark urine = alert nurse. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.critical_threshold: Doctors become concerned when AST is 2 to 3 times above normal (above 80–120 U/L). Currently only slightly elevated. Source: cancer.org Cancer Survivors Network

family_guidance.foods_to_avoid: Avoid alcohol completely — it directly damages the liver. Avoid very fatty or fried foods. Source: cancer.gov
