family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: A high albumin level most often means dehydration — the body does not have enough fluid (for example from severe diarrhea). Source: testing.com
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: Albumin is the main protein in the blood, made by the liver. Low albumin (hypoalbuminemia) means his body is not getting enough protein, OR the liver is producing less, OR chronic inflammation is suppressing it. His albumin of 2.75 is below the normal minimum of 3.5 g/dL. This slows healing, reduces immunity, and affects how medications work in his body. At his age and with his cancer, low albumin is a very serious nutritional concern. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI), search results on albumin in elderly cancer patients
family_guidance.affected_system: The entire body — albumin carries medications, hormones, and nutrients through the blood. Low albumin also allows fluid to leak out of blood vessels, causing swelling in the legs. It significantly worsens healing of any wounds, including pressure sores.
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: Low albumin lets fluid leak out of the blood vessels: watch for swelling in the legs/ankles (press 5 seconds — a lasting dent means fluid) and a swelling belly. Weigh him every morning; a gain of about 1 kg (2-3 lb) in a day is fluid, not fat — report it. IMPORTANT: a belly that swells slowly and feels soft is usually fluid; but a swelling belly WITH sudden severe cramping pain, vomiting, and no gas or stool is a bowel obstruction — an emergency. Source: cancer.gov, cancer.org
family_guidance.warning_signs_tonight: Sudden increased leg swelling, new or worsening wounds that won't close = alert nurse. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.critical_threshold: The normal range is 3.5 to 5.5 g/dL; levels above or below may point to an underlying problem. Source: testing.com
family_guidance.foods_to_give: He urgently needs high protein and calories, but because of the colon tumor the food must be LOW-FIBER (low-residue) to avoid a bowel blockage. GIVE: eggs, soft well-cooked chicken or flaky fish, tofu, smooth (not crunchy) peanut butter, smooth yogurt or cottage cheese, white bread, white rice, peeled mashed potato. Add fortified milk (stir 1 cup powdered milk into 4 cups whole milk) and high-protein shakes (Ensure/Boost) between meals. Offer 5-6 small meals; give drinks between meals, not during. AVOID raw fruit/vegetables, whole grains, nuts, seeds, and beans (they can block a narrowed colon). Source: mskcc.org, cancer.gov
family_guidance.foods_to_avoid: Avoid foods that are high in sugar or empty calories but have no protein: candy, pastries, white bread, soda. These fill him up without helping his albumin or healing. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.hydration_guidance: Offer fluids between meals (not with meals) so he has more appetite for protein-rich food. Soup and broth also provide some protein. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.refusal_handling: If he refuses to eat, prioritize protein above everything else. Even a spoonful of yogurt or a sip of Ensure/Boost counts. Do not force. Offer his favorite foods in very small portions. Report significant food refusal to the nurse so the team can assess IV or tube nutrition. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer — NCI)
family_guidance.bedridden_risks: Very low albumin dramatically increases the risk of pressure sores — the skin breaks down much faster without adequate protein, and wounds heal much more slowly. This is one of the most critical reasons to improve his protein intake. Turn and reposition him every 1 to 2 hours. Ask the nurse if a special pressure-relief mattress is available. Source: healthinaging.org (Caregiver Guide: Skin Problems), cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.supplements_safety: Ask the doctor about oral nutritional supplements (Ensure, Boost, or similar complete nutrition drinks). These are safe and evidence-based for cancer patients. Do not give protein powder or herbal supplements without medical advice. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer — NCI)
family_guidance.immune_and_hygiene: Low albumin weakens immune function and slows the body's ability to fight infections. Strict hand hygiene for all visitors is essential. No sick visitors. Source: cancer.gov (Infection — NCI)
family_guidance.communication_if_confused: Speak in short, simple sentences — one topic at a time. Use a calm, soft, soothing voice. Give him time to respond. If he is confused or scared, gently remind him where he is and that you are there to help. Do not argue, correct, or raise your voice. If he is focused on something upsetting, redirect the conversation to something comforting. Acknowledge his feelings rather than dismissing them. Alert the nurse immediately if confusion is sudden, severe, or new. Source: mskcc.org (Caring for Someone With Delirium)
family_guidance.emotional_support: It can be painful to watch someone you love refuse to eat. The most helpful thing is to offer small amounts of preferred foods without pressure, and to understand that loss of appetite and low albumin are results of his illness, not a choice. At advanced stages, the body naturally reduces hunger — this is normal and not painful. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer — NCI)
family_guidance.oncologist_questions: Ask: 'Is his albumin low enough to affect how his medications work or his ability to tolerate treatment?' and 'Should we arrange nutritional support — a dietitian consult, oral supplements, or IV nutrition (TPN)?' Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer — NCI)
family_guidance.treatment_impact: Very low albumin affects chemotherapy safety — many drugs bind to albumin to travel in the blood, so low albumin changes how drugs are distributed and can increase toxicity or reduce effectiveness. The oncology team may adjust doses or postpone treatment until nutritional status improves. Source: albumin research in cancer patients
family_guidance.palliative_care: Persistently declining albumin despite nutritional support in an advanced cancer patient may indicate the cancer is consuming protein faster than it can be replaced. This is a time to ask the oncologist honestly: 'What are our goals at this stage?' and 'Would nutritional support still make a meaningful difference for him?' Source: cancer.gov (Planning the Transition to End-of-Life Care — NCI)

family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: Hyperchloremia can be caused by severe dehydration, kidney disease, or metabolic acidosis.
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: Low chloride can be caused by fluid loss or certain medications. Managed medically with fluids. Source: testing.com search results
family_guidance.affected_system: Fluid balance and acid-base balance in the body.
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: Weakness, nausea — usually part of a broader electrolyte picture.
family_guidance.hydration_guidance: Follow the doctor's fluid orders. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.refusal_handling: Do not force food, drink, or medications. Offer very small amounts — a few spoonfuls, a few sips — more frequently rather than three large meals. If he refuses solid food, try liquid options: milkshakes, smoothies, soup broth, or oral supplements like Ensure or Boost. If he refuses medication, tell the nurse right away so the care team can decide the next step. At advanced stages, do not force eating or drinking as this can cause discomfort. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer — NCI)
family_guidance.bedridden_risks: Being completely bedridden raises three key risks regardless of this test result: (1) Pressure sores — reposition him every 1 to 2 hours, protect heels, tailbone, and hips with pillows, watch for red or purple skin over bony areas and alert the nurse immediately if found. Do not massage reddened areas. Keep skin clean and dry. (2) Blood clots in the legs (DVT) — do not massage his legs; ask the nurse about compression stockings and gentle foot exercises he can do in bed. (3) Chest infection (pneumonia) — ask the nurse about repositioning him upright at times to help his breathing. Source: mskcc.org (Preventing Pressure Injuries), healthinaging.org (Caregiver Guide: Skin Problems)
family_guidance.immune_and_hygiene: All family members must wash hands thoroughly with soap and warm water before entering his room and after any contact. Anyone with a cold, cough, fever, or any illness must not visit. Avoid bringing in raw or undercooked food from outside. Keep the room clean. Limit the number of visitors at one time. Do not give him fever-reducing medicine (paracetamol, ibuprofen) without asking the nurse first — it can hide signs of a serious infection. Source: cancer.gov (Infection and Neutropenia — NCI)
family_guidance.communication_if_confused: Speak in short, simple sentences — one topic at a time. Use a calm, soft, soothing voice. Give him time to respond. If he is confused or scared, gently remind him where he is and that you are there to help. Do not argue, correct, or raise your voice. If he is focused on something upsetting, redirect the conversation to something comforting. Acknowledge his feelings rather than dismissing them. Alert the nurse immediately if confusion is sudden, severe, or new. Source: mskcc.org (Caring for Someone With Delirium)
family_guidance.emotional_support: Sit with him quietly and let him know you are there — your presence matters more than words. Do not pressure him to eat, talk, or feel positive. Let him direct what he needs. Share simple family news to keep him connected. Avoid sharing distressing news or discussing all his test numbers in detail. If he asks how he is doing, be honest but calm and focus on what the doctors are doing to help him. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer, Fatigue and Cancer)
family_guidance.oncologist_questions: Ask if chloride level is affecting his IV fluid management. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.treatment_impact: Changes in electrolyte levels can show whether a treatment is working and can reveal medication side effects, so this test may track how his body responds to treatment. Source: testing.com

family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: A high calcium (hypercalcemia) means too much calcium in the blood; in cancer this can happen when the cancer spreads to the bones or produces a hormone that raises calcium. Source: testing.com
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: Calcium travels in the blood two ways: bound to protein (albumin), and free (ionized). Because his albumin is very low, his TOTAL calcium reads falsely low — that part is just the missing carrier protein. But ionized calcium measures the active, free calcium directly, and his is truly low. So this is a real calcium deficiency, not just a lab illusion, and it needs the doctor's treatment. Source: cancer.org, mskcc.org
family_guidance.affected_system: Muscles, nerves, and heart function. Ionized calcium is the most active form of calcium.
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: Muscle twitching, tingling or numbness in hands/feet/mouth, muscle cramps, increased confusion. Source: hypocalcemia research
family_guidance.warning_signs_tonight: Muscle spasms he cannot control or tingling around the mouth = alert nurse. Source: hypocalcemia research
family_guidance.critical_threshold: Ionized calcium below 4.0 mg/dL with symptoms = alert nurse immediately. Source: hypocalcemia research
family_guidance.foods_to_give: Dairy products if tolerated — milk, yogurt. Ask the doctor first. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.refusal_handling: Do not force food, drink, or medications. Offer very small amounts — a few spoonfuls, a few sips — more frequently rather than three large meals. If he refuses solid food, try liquid options: milkshakes, smoothies, soup broth, or oral supplements like Ensure or Boost. If he refuses medication, tell the nurse right away so the care team can decide the next step. At advanced stages, do not force eating or drinking as this can cause discomfort. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer — NCI)
family_guidance.bedridden_risks: Being completely bedridden raises three key risks regardless of this test result: (1) Pressure sores — reposition him every 1 to 2 hours, protect heels, tailbone, and hips with pillows, watch for red or purple skin over bony areas and alert the nurse immediately if found. Do not massage reddened areas. Keep skin clean and dry. (2) Blood clots in the legs (DVT) — do not massage his legs; ask the nurse about compression stockings and gentle foot exercises he can do in bed. (3) Chest infection (pneumonia) — ask the nurse about repositioning him upright at times to help his breathing. Source: mskcc.org (Preventing Pressure Injuries), healthinaging.org (Caregiver Guide: Skin Problems)
family_guidance.supplements_safety: Even though his active calcium is low, do NOT buy over-the-counter calcium pills. Too much calcium can cause kidney stones (hard on his fragile kidneys) or swing him to dangerously HIGH calcium (constipation, confusion, heart rhythm problems). Calcium pills also bind his other medicines in the gut and stop them working. He likely also needs vitamin D to absorb calcium — only the doctor should set the dose and timing. Source: cancer.org, mskcc.org
family_guidance.immune_and_hygiene: All family members must wash hands thoroughly with soap and warm water before entering his room and after any contact. Anyone with a cold, cough, fever, or any illness must not visit. Avoid bringing in raw or undercooked food from outside. Keep the room clean. Limit the number of visitors at one time. Do not give him fever-reducing medicine (paracetamol, ibuprofen) without asking the nurse first — it can hide signs of a serious infection. Source: cancer.gov (Infection and Neutropenia — NCI)
family_guidance.communication_if_confused: Speak in short, simple sentences — one topic at a time. Use a calm, soft, soothing voice. Give him time to respond. If he is confused or scared, gently remind him where he is and that you are there to help. Do not argue, correct, or raise your voice. If he is focused on something upsetting, redirect the conversation to something comforting. Acknowledge his feelings rather than dismissing them. Alert the nurse immediately if confusion is sudden, severe, or new. Source: mskcc.org (Caring for Someone With Delirium)
family_guidance.emotional_support: Sit with him quietly and let him know you are there — your presence matters more than words. Do not pressure him to eat, talk, or feel positive. Let him direct what he needs. Share simple family news to keep him connected. Avoid sharing distressing news or discussing all his test numbers in detail. If he asks how he is doing, be honest but calm and focus on what the doctors are doing to help him. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer, Fatigue and Cancer)
family_guidance.oncologist_questions: Ask: 'Does his low ionized calcium require supplementation?' Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.treatment_impact: When treating abnormal calcium, repeat tests check whether the treatment (such as calcium or vitamin D) is working. Source: testing.com

family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: Procalcitonin rises sharply when the body has a serious bacterial infection or sepsis (infection in the bloodstream). His level was critically high (17.84) and has now dropped to 2.37 — this means the antibiotics are working and the infection is less severe than it was. Levels above 2.0 still indicate high risk of sepsis. Source: testing.com (Procalcitonin Test)
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: Normal procalcitonin means no significant bacterial infection is present. Source: testing.com
family_guidance.affected_system: The entire body's response to bacterial infection. PCT is one of the most specific markers of sepsis. Source: testing.com (Procalcitonin Test)
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: Fever or chills, sudden confusion or disorientation, rapid breathing, cold or clammy skin, extreme weakness, very low urine output. These are signs of worsening sepsis. Source: cancer.gov (Infection — NCI)
family_guidance.warning_signs_tonight: Alert the nurse IMMEDIATELY for: fever of 38C or higher, OR a low temperature below 36C; sudden confusion or extreme sleepiness; fast or difficult breathing; cold, clammy, sweaty skin; or very little urine. In the elderly, low body temperature and sudden confusion can be the main signs of worsening sepsis — do not wait for a high fever. Source: cancer.gov, mskcc.org
family_guidance.critical_threshold: Fever of 38C or higher, OR temperature below 36C, in a cancer patient = emergency, call the nurse now. If he is gasping, very confused, cold and clammy, or barely producing urine — in Duhok call ambulance 122 and go to Azadi Teaching Hospital or Duhok Oncology Hospital. Source: cancer.gov, mskcc.org
family_guidance.foods_to_give: High-protein, high-calorie foods to support the immune system and recovery: eggs, chicken, fish, yogurt, Ensure or Boost if solid food is refused. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.foods_to_avoid: Avoid ibuprofen, aspirin, very spicy or acidic foods. Source: cancer.gov (Infection — NCI)
family_guidance.hydration_guidance: Encourage fluids as the doctor allows. With a serious infection, the body needs hydration to fight bacteria and clear toxins. If he has IV fluids, check whether additional oral drinks are allowed. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.refusal_handling: Report refusal to drink to the nurse — with active infection, hydration is medically important and IV fluids may need to be increased. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.bedridden_risks: Active infection combined with immobility increases the risk of pneumonia from secretions pooling in the lungs. Ask the nurse about repositioning him upright periodically and about breathing exercises. Reposition every 1 to 2 hours. Source: mskcc.org, healthinaging.org
family_guidance.supplements_safety: No supplements of any kind without doctor approval during active infection — they can interact dangerously with antibiotics. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.immune_and_hygiene: His immune system is under severe stress from the active infection. Strict hygiene rules: all visitors must wash hands thoroughly before entering. Anyone who is sick must not visit. Limit to immediate family only. Source: cancer.gov (Infection — NCI)
family_guidance.communication_if_confused: Speak in short, simple sentences — one topic at a time. Use a calm, soft, soothing voice. Give him time to respond. If he is confused or scared, gently remind him where he is and that you are there to help. Do not argue, correct, or raise your voice. If he is focused on something upsetting, redirect the conversation to something comforting. Acknowledge his feelings rather than dismissing them. Alert the nurse immediately if confusion is sudden, severe, or new. Source: mskcc.org (Caring for Someone With Delirium)
family_guidance.emotional_support: Share the positive news: his PCT has dropped from 17.84 to 2.37 — this means the antibiotics are working. You can tell him honestly that things are getting better without overwhelming him with numbers. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.oncologist_questions: Ask: 'What specific bacteria is causing the infection?' and 'How long will antibiotics be needed?' and 'At what PCT level is it safe to consider starting or resuming other treatments?' Source: cancer.gov (Infection — NCI)
family_guidance.treatment_impact: Active infection with elevated PCT will delay any chemotherapy, procedures, or surgery until the infection is fully resolved and PCT returns to a safe range. Source: cancer.gov / cancer.net

family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: D-Dimer is a protein fragment released when blood clots break down. His D-Dimer of 4,156 ng/mL is more than 8 times the normal maximum (500 ng/mL). This extremely high level means there is significant blood clotting activity in his body. Cancer itself raises D-Dimer, and being bedridden adds further risk for dangerous clots in the legs (DVT) or lungs (pulmonary embolism). Source: testing.com (D-Dimer Test), cancer.net (Clotting Problems — ASCO)
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: Normal or low D-Dimer means no significant clot formation is occurring. Source: testing.com
family_guidance.affected_system: The blood clotting system. In bedridden patients, clots most commonly form in the deep veins of the legs (DVT). If a clot breaks free and travels to the lungs (pulmonary embolism), it is life-threatening. Source: cancer.net (Clotting Problems — ASCO)
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: Swelling, pain, redness, or warmth in one leg; skin color change in one leg; sudden chest pain; shortness of breath; rapid heartbeat; coughing up blood. Source: cancer.net / cancer.org (Blood Clot Symptoms — ASCO, ACS)
family_guidance.warning_signs_tonight: Alert the nurse IMMEDIATELY if he develops any of these: sudden difficulty breathing, chest pain (worse with breathing or coughing), rapid heartbeat, coughing up blood, or one leg that is suddenly swollen, painful, red, or warm. A pulmonary embolism (clot in the lung) is a life-threatening emergency. Source: cancer.net / cancer.org (Blood Clots)
family_guidance.critical_threshold: Any of these = emergency: sudden shortness of breath, chest pain that worsens with breathing, coughing up blood, a racing heart, fainting, or sudden one-sided leg swelling and pain. Do not wait. In Duhok call ambulance 122 (do not drive him yourself) and go to Azadi Teaching Hospital; bring his medication list, especially any blood thinner. Source: cancer.net, mskcc.org
family_guidance.foods_to_give: No specific foods reduce blood clots. Focus on general good nutrition: protein-rich foods, adequate fluids. Good hydration helps keep blood from thickening. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.foods_to_avoid: If he is started on blood thinners (anticoagulants): avoid large quantities of vitamin K-rich foods (spinach, kale, broccoli, green tea) as these can interfere with certain anticoagulant medications. Always ask the doctor about diet when on blood thinners. Source: cancer.net search results
family_guidance.hydration_guidance: Good hydration is important — dehydration thickens the blood and worsens clotting risk. Encourage fluids as allowed by the doctor. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition — NCI)
family_guidance.refusal_handling: Report fluid refusal to the nurse, as dehydration can worsen blood clot risk. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.bedridden_risks: Being bedridden is a major clot risk. Do NOT massage his legs (it can dislodge a clot). Every 1-2 hours while he is awake, help him do gentle in-bed exercises: ankle pumps (point toes up then down like pressing a pedal), ankle circles, thigh squeezes, buttock squeezes, and heel slides — about 10 of each. Raise his lower legs on a pillow 10-15 cm (above heart level), keep his legs uncrossed, and have him take 3 slow deep belly breaths every hour. Ask the nurse about compression stockings. Check both legs daily — one leg suddenly swollen, red, warm, or painful may be a clot; tell the nurse. Source: mskcc.org, cancer.org
family_guidance.supplements_safety: Do not give fish oil, vitamin E, garlic supplements, or ginkgo biloba — these affect blood clotting and can interact dangerously with anticoagulant medications. Source: cancer.net
family_guidance.immune_and_hygiene: All family members must wash hands thoroughly with soap and warm water before entering his room and after any contact. Anyone with a cold, cough, fever, or any illness must not visit. Avoid bringing in raw or undercooked food from outside. Keep the room clean. Limit the number of visitors at one time. Do not give him fever-reducing medicine (paracetamol, ibuprofen) without asking the nurse first — it can hide signs of a serious infection. Source: cancer.gov (Infection and Neutropenia — NCI)
family_guidance.communication_if_confused: Speak in short, simple sentences — one topic at a time. Use a calm, soft, soothing voice. Give him time to respond. If he is confused or scared, gently remind him where he is and that you are there to help. Do not argue, correct, or raise your voice. If he is focused on something upsetting, redirect the conversation to something comforting. Acknowledge his feelings rather than dismissing them. Alert the nurse immediately if confusion is sudden, severe, or new. Source: mskcc.org (Caring for Someone With Delirium)
family_guidance.emotional_support: This result can be alarming. Reassure him that the doctors are monitoring this closely. Focus the conversation on the care he is receiving rather than the scary numbers. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.oncologist_questions: Ask: 'Is the D-Dimer high enough to start blood thinners (anticoagulants)?' and 'Should we do an ultrasound of his legs to check for an existing blood clot?' and 'What are the specific warning signs we must watch for at the bedside?' Source: cancer.net / cancer.org
family_guidance.treatment_impact: A very high D-Dimer may lead the team to delay certain procedures until clotting risk is assessed. Blood-thinning medications (anticoagulants) may be started, which will require dose monitoring especially given his kidney function. Source: cancer.net / cancer.org
family_guidance.palliative_care: Recurrent blood clots in a cancer patient who is declining despite treatment may prompt a discussion about comfort-focused care. Ask the team: 'What are we hoping to achieve with clot treatment at this stage?' Source: cancer.gov (Planning the Transition to End-of-Life Care — NCI)

family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: HbA1c of 6.7% measures the average blood sugar over the past 2–3 months. A result of 6.7% is in the diabetes range (normal is below 5.8%). Cancer treatment can raise blood sugar levels, and high blood sugar slows healing and increases infection risk. Source: mskcc.org (Diabetes Management Guide)
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: A result under 5.7% is considered normal, meaning blood sugar has been well controlled. Source: testing.com
family_guidance.affected_system: Blood sugar regulation. High blood sugar slows wound healing, increases infection risk, and can cause confusion.
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: Signs of high blood sugar: excessive thirst, dry mouth, frequent urination, blurry vision, drowsiness, wounds that heal more slowly than usual. Source: mskcc.org (Diabetes Management Guide)
family_guidance.warning_signs_tonight: Alert the team if blood sugar drops below 70 (shaky, sweaty, confused) or rises above 400 (very thirsty, confused, urinating a lot). A sweet, fruity smell on his breath together with sudden confusion is a serious emergency (DKA) — get help immediately. Source: mskcc.org
family_guidance.critical_threshold: Blood glucose below 70 mg/dL or above 400 mg/dL = contact medical team immediately. MSK's target range for cancer patients: 100–200 mg/dL. Source: mskcc.org (Diabetes Management Guide)
family_guidance.foods_to_give: Non-starchy vegetables (fill half the plate when possible), lean proteins (chicken, fish, eggs), high-fiber foods (oats, beans, lentils). Source: mskcc.org (Diabetes Management Guide)
family_guidance.foods_to_avoid: Avoid regular soda, fruit juice with added sugar, candy, pastries, white bread, and refined grains. Source: mskcc.org (Diabetes Management Guide)
family_guidance.hydration_guidance: Drink water or unsweetened drinks. Avoid fruit juices and sugary drinks. Good hydration helps the body manage blood sugar. Source: mskcc.org (Diabetes Management Guide)
family_guidance.refusal_handling: Do not force food, drink, or medications. Offer very small amounts — a few spoonfuls, a few sips — more frequently rather than three large meals. If he refuses solid food, try liquid options: milkshakes, smoothies, soup broth, or oral supplements like Ensure or Boost. If he refuses medication, tell the nurse right away so the care team can decide the next step. At advanced stages, do not force eating or drinking as this can cause discomfort. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer — NCI)
family_guidance.bedridden_risks: High blood sugar in a bedridden patient greatly increases the risk of skin infections and slow-healing pressure sores. Inspect his skin carefully during repositioning. Source: mskcc.org (Diabetes Management Guide), healthinaging.org
family_guidance.supplements_safety: Do not give chromium, cinnamon, or any 'blood sugar lowering' herbal supplements without doctor approval. These can interact unpredictably with cancer treatment. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.immune_and_hygiene: All family members must wash hands thoroughly with soap and warm water before entering his room and after any contact. Anyone with a cold, cough, fever, or any illness must not visit. Avoid bringing in raw or undercooked food from outside. Keep the room clean. Limit the number of visitors at one time. Do not give him fever-reducing medicine (paracetamol, ibuprofen) without asking the nurse first — it can hide signs of a serious infection. Source: cancer.gov (Infection and Neutropenia — NCI)
family_guidance.communication_if_confused: Speak in short, simple sentences — one topic at a time. Use a calm, soft, soothing voice. Give him time to respond. If he is confused or scared, gently remind him where he is and that you are there to help. Do not argue, correct, or raise your voice. If he is focused on something upsetting, redirect the conversation to something comforting. Acknowledge his feelings rather than dismissing them. Alert the nurse immediately if confusion is sudden, severe, or new. Source: mskcc.org (Caring for Someone With Delirium)
family_guidance.emotional_support: Sit with him quietly and let him know you are there — your presence matters more than words. Do not pressure him to eat, talk, or feel positive. Let him direct what he needs. Share simple family news to keep him connected. Avoid sharing distressing news or discussing all his test numbers in detail. If he asks how he is doing, be honest but calm and focus on what the doctors are doing to help him. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer, Fatigue and Cancer)
family_guidance.oncologist_questions: Ask: 'Is his high HbA1c related to his cancer treatment?' and 'Does he need any medication to manage his blood sugar during treatment?' and 'What blood sugar range are you targeting for him?' Source: mskcc.org (Diabetes Management Guide)
family_guidance.treatment_impact: Important: do NOT put him on a strict diabetic diet or cut his food to lower this number. He has muscle wasting; starving him to fix a mildly high sugar would speed up weight and muscle loss, which is more dangerous than a slightly high sugar. A relaxed sugar target is safer for him. Do NOT increase his diabetes medicine on your own — in a frail elderly patient, low blood sugar (which he may not even feel) can cause fainting and a deadly fall. Steroids given with chemo also push sugar up. Let the oncology team manage it. Source: mskcc.org, cancer.gov

family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: High bilirubin causes jaundice (yellowing of skin and eyes) and can indicate liver problems or bile duct blockage. Currently at the upper limit of normal — monitor closely. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: Low bilirubin is normal and healthy. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.affected_system: Liver and bile ducts. Bilirubin is produced when red blood cells break down.
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: Yellowing of the skin or whites of the eyes, dark yellow-brown urine (like strong tea), pale or clay-colored stools, itchy skin, or upper right abdominal pain. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.warning_signs_tonight: If skin or eyes turn yellow, or urine becomes very dark brown = alert nurse immediately. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.critical_threshold: Bilirubin rising above 1.2 mg/dL toward 2+ mg/dL with yellow eyes or skin = alert nurse. Source: cancer.org Cancer Survivors Network
family_guidance.foods_to_give: Light easily digested foods. No fatty or fried foods. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.foods_to_avoid: No alcohol. Avoid very fatty or fried foods. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.hydration_guidance: Good hydration helps the liver process bilirubin. 8+ cups daily. Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.refusal_handling: Do not force food, drink, or medications. Offer very small amounts — a few spoonfuls, a few sips — more frequently rather than three large meals. If he refuses solid food, try liquid options: milkshakes, smoothies, soup broth, or oral supplements like Ensure or Boost. If he refuses medication, tell the nurse right away so the care team can decide the next step. At advanced stages, do not force eating or drinking as this can cause discomfort. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer — NCI)

Do not massage reddened areas. Keep skin clean and dry. (2) Blood clots in the legs (DVT) — do not massage his legs; ask the nurse about compression stockings and gentle foot exercises he can do in bed. (3) Chest infection (pneumonia) — ask the nurse about repositioning him upright at times to help his breathing. Source: mskcc.org (Preventing Pressure Injuries), healthinaging.org (Caregiver Guide: Skin Problems)

Anyone with a cold, cough, fever, or any illness must not visit. Avoid bringing in raw or undercooked food from outside. Keep the room clean. Limit the number of visitors at one time. If he is confused or scared, gently remind him where he is and that you are there to help. Do not argue, correct, or raise your voice. If he is focused on something upsetting, redirect the conversation to something comforting. Acknowledge his feelings rather than dismissing them. Alert the nurse immediately if confusion is sudden, severe, or new. Source: mskcc.org (Caring for Someone With Delirium)
family_guidance.emotional_support: Sit with him quietly and let him know you are there — your presence matters more than words. Do not pressure him to eat, talk, or feel positive. Let him direct what he needs. Share simple family news to keep him connected. Avoid sharing distressing news or discussing all his test numbers in detail. If he asks how he is doing, be honest but calm and focus on what the doctors are doing to help him. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer, Fatigue and Cancer)
family_guidance.oncologist_questions: Ask: 'Is bilirubin trending upward and could it affect his liver or treatment?' Source: cancer.gov
family_guidance.treatment_impact: Rising bilirubin can delay chemotherapy and may indicate liver involvement by cancer. Source: cancer.gov

family_guidance.meaning_high_simple: Can indicate abnormal platelet activation or destruction, often seen in vascular diseases, inflammatory conditions, or certain cancers.
family_guidance.meaning_low_simple: Generally indicates uniform platelet size, which is typically normal and not clinically significant.
family_guidance.affected_system: Platelet size distribution. Currently normal.
family_guidance.symptoms_to_watch: No direct symptoms. Watch platelet count alongside this value.
family_guidance.foods_to_avoid: No aspirin or ibuprofen. Source: cancer.org
family_guidance.refusal_handling: Do not force food, drink, or medications. Offer very small amounts — a few spoonfuls, a few sips — more frequently rather than three large meals. If he refuses solid food, try liquid options: milkshakes, smoothies, soup broth, or oral supplements like Ensure or Boost. If he refuses medication, tell the nurse right away so the care team can decide the next step. At advanced stages, do not force eating or drinking as this can cause discomfort. Source: cancer.gov (Nutrition During Cancer — NCI)
family_guidance.bedridden_risks: Being completely bedridden raises three key risks regardless of this test result: (1) Pressure sores — reposition him every 1 to 2 hours, protect heels, tailbone, and hips with pillows, watch for red or purple skin over bony areas and alert the nurse immediately if found. Do not massage reddened areas. Keep skin clean and dry. (2) Blood clots in the legs (DVT) — do not massage his legs; ask the nurse about compression stockings and gentle foot exercises he can do in bed. (3) Chest infection (pneumonia) — ask the nurse about repositioning him upright at times to help his breathing. Source: mskcc.org (Preventing Pressure Injuries), healthinaging.org (Caregiver Guide: Skin Problems)
family_guidance.immune_and_hygiene: All family members must wash hands thoroughly with soap and warm water before entering his room and after any contact. Anyone with a cold, cough, fever, or any illness must not visit. Avoid bringing in raw or undercooked food from outside. Keep the room clean. Limit the number of visitors at one time. Do not give him fever-reducing medicine (paracetamol, ibuprofen) without asking the nurse first — it can hide signs of a serious infection. Source: cancer.gov (Infection and Neutropenia — NCI)
